notes.note: The letter-heading. The recipient is the younger P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus (cos. 48 BC with Caesar, proconsul of Asia 46-44 BC), Cicero's fellow augur; this is one of a short cluster of recommendations Cicero addressed to him during his Asian governorship.

notes.anchor: A. Caecina to you --- a client most especially attached to your family
notes.note: Aulus Caecina of Volaterrae, an Etruscan man of letters and son of the defendant of Cicero's Pro Caecina; a Pompeian in the civil war, he had written a pamphlet against Caesar and was living in exile. A 'client' (cliens) of the Servilii by hereditary tie, he hoped the family's patronage and the governor's protection would shelter him.

notes.anchor: relying on the clemency of your colleague
notes.note: The 'colleague' is Julius Caesar, Servilius's fellow consul of 48 BC; only Caesar's clemency (clementia) could grant Caecina the full 'restoration' (salus) from his proscribed condition. Cicero hopes for it but in the meantime asks Servilius for protection in the province.

notes.anchor: your province to be the safest harbour for himself
notes.note: Servilius's province was Asia, where he served as proconsul 46-44 BC. An exile such as Caecina, debarred from Italy, sought refuge and the means to wind up his business affairs in a friendly governor's jurisdiction.
